summary: Gruber dismantles a Wall Street Journal article claiming Apple deliberately cultivated iMessage's blue-vs-green bubble distinction to lock in teenage users. He corrects numerous technical errors in the piece — SMS was always green before iMessage existed, and limitations like missing typing indicators are SMS protocol constraints, not Apple spite. He argues the U.S.-centric framing ignores that iMessage is a global outlier, with WhatsApp, Telegram, and others dominating elsewhere. The real explanation for teen iPhone preference is simpler: iPhones are better products, and teenagers care more about quality than adults give them credit for.
key_points: SMS messages were rendered green in iOS before iMessage even existed — the color distinction wasn't a deliberate competitive weapon against Android
Missing features in SMS conversations (typing indicators, reactions) are protocol limitations, not features Apple withholds out of spite
iMessage's U.S. dominance is explained by carriers making SMS free, removing the incentive to adopt alternatives like WhatsApp that dominate elsewhere
The rich ecosystem of cross-platform messaging apps on iOS (WhatsApp, Telegram, Signal, Discord) directly contradicts the 'stifling competition' narrative
Teenagers prefer iPhones because they're better products, not because they're seduced by blue bubbles — the WSJ has the causation backwards
money_quotes: The whole point of Apple — the entire company — is to offer superior products and services to customers willing to pay for them.

A much simpler nutshell explanation is that teenagers have a keener sense of cool, and care more about what's cool, than adults. And the iPhone always has been and remains today cooler than any Android phone.
People — including teenagers — don't buy iPhones because iMessage is cool or good. People use iMessage because iPhones are cool and good.
The truth is, SMS sucks and iMessage has features SMS never will or could.
If text bubble color was all that mattered, everyone could switch to Android and their SMS messages would all be blue.
There's nothing wrong with green. Green means go. Green is money.
I'll go out on a limb here and say that an analyst who's willing to project which devices today's teenagers are going to be using 50 years from now is not an analyst you should be quoting.
What a pile of clickbait horseshit.
key_insight: The WSJ's iMessage-as-teen-trap narrative inverts causation: teenagers don't choose iPhones for blue bubbles — iMessage is popular because iPhones are genuinely better products, and SMS limitations are protocol constraints, not competitive spite.
